era_context: The year 1808 falls at the height of the Napoleonic era, when French imperial power dominated continental Europe through a combination of military conquest, dynastic manipulation, and the Continental System's economic warfare against Britain. The period saw the emergence of popular nationalist resistance against French hegemony, most dramatically in Spain and Portugal. Globally, the Atlantic slave trade was under increasing legal challenge, the Ottoman Empire was wracked by internal power struggles, and the transfer of the Portuguese court to Brazil initiated a transformation of South American colonial politics. The Qing dynasty under the Jiaqing Emperor continued consolidating power after the long Qianlong reign, while British India expanded under Company rule.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa beyond coastal trading posts and the Ethiopian highlands — interior political developments are poorly documented in European sources and oral traditions for this specific year.
Southeast Asia — the Konbaung dynasty in Burma, Siam under Rama II, and the Vietnamese Nguyen-Tay Son conflicts are underrepresented in English-language historiography for this year.
Oceania and Pacific Islands beyond the Australian penal colonies.
Central Asian khanates — Bukhara, Khiva, and Kokand are poorly covered in available sources for this year.

events.title: US ban on the Atlantic slave trade takes effect
events.description: The Act Prohibiting Importation of Slaves, signed by President Jefferson on 2 March 1807, took effect on 1 January 1808 — the earliest date permitted under Article I, Section 9 of the US Constitution. The law made it illegal to import enslaved persons into the United States from foreign nations, though domestic slavery and the internal slave trade continued unabated. Enforcement was chronically weak, and illegal smuggling of enslaved Africans continued for decades. Britain had abolished its own slave trade in March 1807, and the near-simultaneous Anglo-American prohibitions marked a significant shift in Atlantic world politics, though neither law addressed the institution of slavery itself.
key_figures: Thomas Jefferson (President of the United States)

sources.name: Act Prohibiting Importation of Slaves (2 Stat. 426, 9th Congress)
W.E.B. Du Bois, The Suppression of the African Slave-Trade to the United States of America, 1638–1870 (1896)

events.certainty_note: Federal legislation with precise effective date. Implementation and enforcement are well-documented through customs records and congressional debates.

events.title: Portuguese royal court transfers to Brazil
events.description: The Portuguese royal family and court, numbering between 10,000 and 15,000 people, arrived in Salvador, Bahia in January 1808 and relocated to Rio de Janeiro in March, having fled Lisbon in November 1807 ahead of Junot's French invasion. Prince Regent João (ruling for his incapacitated mother Maria I) immediately opened Brazilian ports to friendly nations on 28 January, effectively ending the colonial trade monopoly. This act, along with subsequent decrees permitting manufacturing and establishing institutions of higher learning, fundamentally altered Brazil's colonial status. The transfer made Rio de Janeiro the de facto capital of the Portuguese Empire — the only instance of a European colonial metropole governing from one of its colonies — and set in motion the political trajectory toward Brazilian independence in 1822.
key_figures: Prince Regent João (later João VI of Portugal)
Queen Maria I of Portugal (incapacitated)
Lord Strangford (British ambassador who facilitated the crossing)

sources.name: Decree of 28 January 1808 opening Brazilian ports (Carta Régia)
Laurentino Gomes, 1808: The Flight of the Emperor (2007)
Kenneth Maxwell, Conflicts and Conspiracies: Brazil and Portugal, 1750–1808 (1973)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented through Portuguese royal archives, British diplomatic correspondence, and Brazilian colonial records.

events.title: Abdications of Bayonne — Napoleon seizes the Spanish crown
events.description: In a sequence of coerced abdications at Bayonne in April–May 1808, Napoleon forced both Ferdinand VII and his father Charles IV to renounce their claims to the Spanish throne. Ferdinand had already displaced his father in the Tumult of Aranjuez (17–19 March), but Napoleon summoned both to Bayonne and compelled Ferdinand to return the crown to Charles, who then ceded it to Napoleon. Napoleon subsequently installed his brother Joseph Bonaparte as King of Spain on 6 June. The Bayonne abdications destroyed the legitimacy of French rule in Spanish eyes and became the catalyst for the nationwide uprising that launched the Peninsular War, as well as for independence movements across Spanish America.
key_figures: Napoleon Bonaparte (Emperor of the French)
Ferdinand VII (King of Spain, abdicated under duress)
Charles IV (former King of Spain)
Joseph Bonaparte (installed as King of Spain)
Manuel Godoy (Chief Minister, whose unpopularity triggered the Aranjuez crisis)

sources.name: Abdication documents at Bayonne, May 1808 (Spanish royal archives)
Charles Esdaile, The Peninsular War: A New History (2003)
Correspondence de Napoléon Ier, Vol. XVII

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented through French imperial correspondence, Spanish state papers, and diplomatic dispatches from multiple nations.

events.title: Dos de Mayo uprising in Madrid
events.description: On 2 May 1808, the people of Madrid rose spontaneously against the French garrison occupying the city, triggered by the attempt to remove the last Bourbon royals to Bayonne. The uprising was suppressed with extreme brutality by Murat's troops, including Mameluke cavalry charges and mass executions of prisoners on 3 May. The events — later immortalised in Goya's paintings El dos de mayo and El tres de mayo — became the symbolic origin of the Spanish War of Independence and catalysed insurrections across the country. While militarily insignificant, the Dos de Mayo became one of the most powerful symbols of popular resistance to Napoleonic imperialism.
key_figures: Joachim Murat (French commander in Madrid)
Daoíz and Velarde (Spanish artillery officers killed defending the Monteleón barracks)

sources.name: French military dispatches from Madrid, May 1808
Francisco Goya, El dos de mayo de 1808 and El tres de mayo de 1808 (painted 1814, depicting events of 1808)
David Gates, The Spanish Ulcer: A History of the Peninsular War (1986)

events.certainty_note: Attested by multiple contemporary sources including French military records, Spanish eyewitness accounts, and diplomatic correspondence.

events.title: Battle of Bailén — first major Napoleonic field defeat
events.description: On 19 July 1808, a Spanish army under General Francisco Castaños defeated and forced the surrender of a French corps of approximately 17,600 men under General Pierre Dupont at Bailén in Andalusia. This was the first time a Napoleonic army had surrendered in open battle, shattering the myth of French invincibility and electrifying resistance movements across Europe. The defeat forced Joseph Bonaparte to abandon Madrid temporarily and compelled Napoleon to intervene personally in Spain later that year. The psychological impact far exceeded the tactical significance, demonstrating that French armies could be beaten by determined national resistance.
key_figures: Francisco Castaños (Spanish commanding general)
Pierre Dupont de l'Étang (French general, surrendered)
Theodor von Reding (Swiss-born Spanish general)

sources.name: French and Spanish military after-action reports, July 1808
Charles Esdaile, The Peninsular War: A New History (2003)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented battle with surviving military records from both sides.

events.title: British intervention in Portugal — Battle of Vimeiro and Convention of Cintra
events.description: A British expeditionary force under Sir Arthur Wellesley (the future Duke of Wellington) landed in Portugal in August 1808 and defeated Junot's French army at the Battle of Vimeiro on 21 August. However, Wellesley's superiors negotiated the controversial Convention of Cintra (30 August), which allowed the French to evacuate Portugal with their arms and plundered property aboard British ships. The convention caused public outrage in Britain and led to a formal inquiry, but the campaign nonetheless liberated Portugal and established the British military presence that would anchor the Peninsular War for the next six years.
key_figures: Sir Arthur Wellesley (British commander at Vimeiro)
Sir Hew Dalrymple (British commander who signed the Convention)
Jean-Andoche Junot (French commander in Portugal)

sources.name: Wellesley's dispatches, August 1808 (published in Supplementary Despatches)
Rory Muir, Britain and the Defeat of Napoleon, 1807–1815 (1996)

events.certainty_note: Richly documented through British parliamentary papers, military dispatches, and the subsequent court of inquiry records.

events.title: Congress of Erfurt — Napoleon and Alexander I reaffirm alliance
events.description: Napoleon and Tsar Alexander I met at Erfurt from 27 September to 14 October 1808 to reaffirm the Franco-Russian alliance established at Tilsit in 1807. Napoleon sought Russian support for his Spanish campaign and a guarantee against Austrian aggression, while Alexander sought French acquiescence to Russian expansion against the Ottoman Empire. The outward display of amity masked growing Russian disillusionment with the Continental System and Talleyrand's secret encouragement of Alexander to resist Napoleon — a betrayal that would contribute to the eventual Franco-Russian rupture. The convention signed at Erfurt was less substantive than Napoleon had hoped.
key_figures: Napoleon Bonaparte (Emperor of the French)
Alexander I (Tsar of Russia)
Charles Maurice de Talleyrand (secretly advising Alexander against Napoleon)

sources.name: Convention of Erfurt, 12 October 1808
Talleyrand, Mémoires du Prince de Talleyrand (published posthumously, accounts of Erfurt)
Adam Zamoyski, Napoleon: A Life (2018)

events.certainty_note: Documented through official diplomatic records, correspondence of both emperors, and multiple memoir accounts.

events.title: Finnish War — Russia invades Swedish Finland
events.description: In February 1808, Russia invaded Finland (then part of Sweden) as a consequence of the Treaty of Tilsit and Swedish refusal to join the Continental System against Britain. Russian forces under General Buxhoevden crossed the border on 21 February and captured the fortress of Sveaborg (Suomenlinna) on 3 May after a controversial surrender. Despite spirited Finnish-Swedish guerrilla resistance and several tactical victories in the interior, the war would ultimately result in Sweden's loss of Finland to Russia by the Treaty of Fredrikshamn in 1809. This war ended over 600 years of Finnish integration with Sweden and began the era of the Grand Duchy of Finland under Russian rule.
key_figures: Gustav IV Adolf (King of Sweden)
Friedrich Wilhelm von Buxhoevden (Russian commanding general)
Carl Olof Cronstedt (Swedish admiral who surrendered Sveaborg)

sources.name: Russian and Swedish military records, 1808
Jason Lavery, The History of Finland (2006)

events.certainty_note: Well-documented through military archives of both belligerents and Finnish regional records.

events.title: Ottoman coup — Mahmud II becomes Sultan
events.description: The reformist Grand Vizier Alemdar Mustafa Pasha marched on Constantinople in July 1808 with his Danubian army to restore the deposed Sultan Selim III, but arrived to find Selim already murdered on the orders of Sultan Mustafa IV. Alemdar placed Mahmud II, the sole surviving Ottoman prince, on the throne on 28 July. Alemdar became Grand Vizier and attempted military reforms through the Deed of Alliance (Sened-i İttifak) signed in October, a document sometimes called the Ottoman Magna Carta for its attempt to define mutual obligations between sultan and provincial notables. However, a Janissary revolt in November killed Alemdar and destroyed the reform programme, though Mahmud II survived and would eventually achieve the destruction of the Janissaries in 1826.
key_figures: Mahmud II (new Sultan of the Ottoman Empire)
Alemdar Mustafa Pasha (Grand Vizier, killed November 1808)
Selim III (deposed Sultan, murdered before restoration)
Mustafa IV (Sultan, deposed by Alemdar)

sources.name: Sened-i İttifak (Deed of Alliance), October 1808
Stanford Shaw, History of the Ottoman Empire and Modern Turkey, Vol. II (1977)
Contemporary Ottoman chronicle accounts (Cevdet Paşa, Tarih-i Cevdet, compiled later from contemporary sources)

events.certainty_note: Well-attested through Ottoman court chronicles, foreign diplomatic dispatches, and the surviving text of the Sened-i İttifak.

events.title: Rum Rebellion in New South Wales
events.description: On 26 January 1808, officers of the New South Wales Corps, led by Major George Johnston at the instigation of John Macarthur, arrested and deposed Governor William Bligh — the same Bligh of the 1789 Bounty mutiny. The rebellion, known as the Rum Rebellion, was the only successful armed takeover of government in Australian history. It arose from disputes over the rum trade, land grants, and Bligh's attempts to curtail the economic power of the military officer corps. Johnston ruled as lieutenant-governor until the arrival of Governor Lachlan Macquarie in 1810. The incident highlighted the lawless character of early colonial governance and led to significant administrative reforms.
key_figures: William Bligh (Governor of New South Wales, deposed)
George Johnston (Major, NSW Corps, led the arrest)
John Macarthur (colonial entrepreneur and instigator)

sources.name: Records of the Judge Advocate's office, NSW, 1808
Court-martial of George Johnston, London, 1811 (transcripts)
Alan Atkinson, The Europeans in Australia, Vol. 2 (2004)

events.certainty_note: Richly documented through colonial administrative records, court-martial proceedings, and personal correspondence.

events.title: Dalton publishes A New System of Chemical Philosophy
events.description: John Dalton published the first part of A New System of Chemical Philosophy in 1808, providing the first systematic scientific exposition of atomic theory. The work proposed that each chemical element is composed of indivisible atoms of characteristic weight, that compounds are formed by combinations of atoms in simple whole-number ratios, and provided the first table of relative atomic weights. While Dalton's specific weight measurements were often inaccurate and his rule of 'greatest simplicity' for inferring molecular formulae was flawed, the conceptual framework of quantitative atomic theory became foundational to modern chemistry and physics.
key_figures: John Dalton (chemist and natural philosopher)

sources.name: John Dalton, A New System of Chemical Philosophy, Part I (1808)
Frank Greenaway, John Dalton and the Atom (1966)

events.certainty_note: Published work with precise date. The book survives in multiple copies.

events.title: Humphry Davy isolates alkaline earth metals
events.description: Building on his 1807 isolation of sodium and potassium through electrolysis, Sir Humphry Davy used electrolytic methods in 1808 to isolate barium, strontium, calcium, and magnesium as distinct metallic elements. He also isolated boron (independently of Gay-Lussac and Thénard in France). These discoveries, performed at the Royal Institution in London, demonstrated the power of electrolysis as an analytical tool and dramatically expanded the known periodic table. Davy's work showed that substances long considered elemental (such as lime and magnesia) were in fact compounds, overturning centuries of chemical classification.
key_figures: Humphry Davy (chemist, Royal Institution)
Joseph Louis Gay-Lussac (French chemist, independent boron work)
Louis Jacques Thénard (French chemist, collaborated with Gay-Lussac)

sources.name: Humphry Davy, 'Electro-chemical researches on the decomposition of the earths', Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society (1808)
David Knight, Humphry Davy: Science and Power (1992)

events.certainty_note: Published in Philosophical Transactions with precise dates of experiments. Corroborated by Royal Institution laboratory notebooks.

events.title: Beethoven premieres Fifth and Sixth Symphonies
events.description: On 22 December 1808, Ludwig van Beethoven premiered his Symphony No. 5 in C minor and Symphony No. 6 in F major (the 'Pastoral') at the Theater an der Wien in Vienna, in a mammoth four-hour concert that also included the Choral Fantasy and the Fourth Piano Concerto. The Fifth Symphony, with its iconic four-note opening motif, became perhaps the most recognisable symphonic work in Western music and a touchstone for Romantic-era ideas about struggle and triumph. The Pastoral Symphony pioneered programmatic orchestral music with its depictions of nature. The premiere itself was by many accounts chaotic — the hall was freezing, rehearsals inadequate — but the works would transform the symphonic tradition.
key_figures: Ludwig van Beethoven (composer and pianist)

sources.name: Johann Friedrich Reichardt, eyewitness account of the concert (Vertraute Briefe, 1809)
Jan Swafford, Beethoven: Anguish and Triumph (2014)

events.certainty_note: Documented through concert programmes, Beethoven's correspondence, and multiple eyewitness accounts.

events.title: Junta movements across Spanish America
events.description: News of the Bayonne abdications and the Spanish uprising against Napoleon reached the Americas in mid-1808, triggering the first junta movements in Spanish colonial territories. While colonial elites initially proclaimed loyalty to Ferdinand VII against the French usurper, the crisis of legitimate authority opened the question of sovereignty: if the king was captive, where did governing authority reside? Attempts at autonomous juntas were made in Mexico City (led by the Ayuntamiento) and Montevideo, among others, though most were suppressed by viceregal authorities in 1808. These early movements established the legal and ideological frameworks that would drive full independence movements from 1810 onward.
key_figures: José de Iturrigaray (Viceroy of New Spain, deposed by Spanish loyalists)
Francisco Javier de Elío (led loyalist junta in Montevideo)

sources.name: Actas del Ayuntamiento de México, 1808
Jaime E. Rodríguez O., The Independence of Spanish America (1998)

events.certainty_note: Documented through colonial administrative records, Audiencia proceedings, and contemporary correspondence.

events.title: Sierra Leone becomes a British Crown Colony
events.description: On 1 January 1808, Sierra Leone was transferred from the failing Sierra Leone Company to direct British Crown governance, becoming a Crown Colony. The colony had been founded in 1787 as a settlement for freed slaves and 'Black Poor' from London, and later received thousands of 'Nova Scotian' Black Loyalists and Jamaican Maroons. As a Crown Colony, it became a base for the Royal Navy's West Africa Squadron tasked with suppressing the slave trade following the 1807 Abolition Act. Freetown would develop into a major centre of West African colonial administration and the hub of recaptive (liberated African) resettlement throughout the nineteenth century.
key_figures: Thomas Perronet Thompson (first Crown Colony governor, arriving 1808)

sources.name: British Parliamentary papers on the Sierra Leone Company transfer, 1807–1808
Christopher Fyfe, A History of Sierra Leone (1962)

events.certainty_note: Documented through British colonial office records and parliamentary acts.

events.title: Napoleon issues the Decree of Chámartin — reorganisation of Spain
events.description: Napoleon personally led a massive army into Spain in November 1808, sweeping aside Spanish resistance at the battles of Espinosa, Gamonal, Tudela, and Somosierra. He entered Madrid on 4 December and from the suburb of Chamartín issued a series of decrees abolishing the Spanish Inquisition, feudal rights, and internal customs barriers, and reducing the number of religious houses by two-thirds. While these reforms reflected Enlightenment principles, their imposition by a foreign conqueror undermined their legitimacy. Napoleon's direct intervention stabilised French control temporarily but failed to suppress the guerrilla resistance that would drain French resources for years.
key_figures: Napoleon Bonaparte (Emperor of the French)
Joseph Bonaparte (titular King of Spain, restored to Madrid)

sources.name: Décrets de Chamartín, December 1808 (Correspondance de Napoléon Ier, Vol. XVIII)
Charles Esdaile, The Peninsular War: A New History (2003)

events.certainty_note: Official French imperial decrees survive in full text. Military campaign documented through extensive military records.

events.title: Jiaqing Emperor consolidates power in Qing China
events.description: The Jiaqing Emperor, who had nominally acceded in 1796 but only gained real power after the death of his father the Qianlong Emperor in 1799, continued efforts in 1808 to reverse the institutional corruption of the late Qianlong era. He dealt with ongoing White Lotus Rebellion aftermath and piracy on the southeast coast. British ships were increasingly pressing for expanded trade access beyond the Canton system, and in 1808 HMS Phaeton entered Nagasaki harbour (a Japanese port within the broader East Asian trading system) in pursuit of Dutch vessels, an incident that alarmed Qing officials about European naval aggression in the region. The broader context was one of imperial overstretch and the early signs of dynastic decline.
key_figures: Jiaqing Emperor (Qing Emperor of China)

sources.name: Qing Shilu (Veritable Records of the Qing dynasty), Jiaqing reign entries
William T. Rowe, China's Last Empire: The Great Qing (2009)

events.certainty_note: Documented through Qing imperial records, though official court chronicles tend to minimise internal problems.

events.title: Fichte delivers Addresses to the German Nation
events.description: Philosopher Johann Gottlieb Fichte delivered his Reden an die deutsche Nation (Addresses to the German Nation) as a series of public lectures in French-occupied Berlin between December 1807 and March 1808. The lectures called for German cultural and spiritual regeneration through education and national consciousness in response to Prussian defeat and French domination. While their immediate audience was small, the Addresses became foundational texts of German nationalism and were retrospectively elevated to canonical status during the unification movements of the mid-nineteenth century. They represent one of the earliest systematic articulations of cultural nationalism as a political programme.
key_figures: Johann Gottlieb Fichte (philosopher)

sources.name: Johann Gottlieb Fichte, Reden an die deutsche Nation (published 1808)
Anthony J. La Vopa, Fichte: The Self and the Calling of Philosophy (2001)

events.certainty_note: Published text with known lecture dates. Contemporary reception documented through Berlin press and private correspondence.

events.title: Goethe publishes Faust, Part One
events.description: Johann Wolfgang von Goethe published the first part of Faust (Faust: Der Tragödie erster Teil) in 1808, after decades of development from the earlier Urfaust fragment. The work, dramatising the scholar Faust's pact with the devil Mephistopheles and his tragic affair with Gretchen, became the supreme masterwork of German literature and one of the defining texts of European Romanticism. Its exploration of the tension between knowledge and experience, reason and passion, established themes that would resonate through nineteenth-century philosophy and literature.
key_figures: Johann Wolfgang von Goethe (author)

sources.name: Johann Wolfgang von Goethe, Faust: Der Tragödie erster Teil (1808, published by Cotta)
Nicholas Boyle, Goethe: The Poet and the Age, Vol. II (2000)

events.certainty_note: Published work with known publication date and surviving publisher correspondence.

events.title: South Chinese sea piracy confederation at its peak
events.description: The pirate confederation operating in the South China Sea, led by the Red Flag Fleet under Zheng Yi and (after his death in 1807) his widow Zheng Yi Sao (Ching Shih), reached its peak strength around 1808 with an estimated 1,500–1,800 vessels and 70,000–80,000 followers. This was arguably the largest pirate fleet in world history, controlling much of the Guangdong coastal trade and extracting protection fees from fishing and merchant vessels. The Qing navy proved unable to suppress the confederation, which operated under a strict internal code of laws. It would not be broken until Zheng Yi Sao negotiated a mass amnesty in 1810.
key_figures: Zheng Yi Sao / Ching Shih (pirate leader, Red Flag Fleet)
Zhang Bao (lieutenant and eventual co-leader)

sources.name: Richard Glasspoole, A Brief Narrative of My Captivity and Treatment Amongst the Ladrones (1812, eyewitness account from 1809–1810 captivity)
Dian Murray, Pirates of the South China Coast, 1790–1810 (1987)
Qing provincial memorials from Guangdong, 1808

events.certainty_note: Documented through Qing government records, European captive accounts, and Portuguese Macau archives. Fleet size estimates vary but the order of magnitude is well established.

disconfirming_evidence: Several points of complication for events listed: (1) The Battle of Bailén (1808-005) is often called the 'first defeat of a Napoleonic army,' but this framing is contested — French forces had suffered tactical reverses before (e.g., at Acre in 1799 and in Saint-Domingue in 1802–03). Bailén was the first surrender of a major French field force in a European pitched battle, which is a more precise claim. (2) The Rum Rebellion (1808-010) is sometimes portrayed as a popular uprising, but modern scholarship emphasises it was essentially a coup by economic elites defending their monopoly interests, not a democratic movement. (3) Fichte's Addresses (1808-018) are sometimes presented as an immediate catalyst of German nationalism, but their contemporary audience was small and their elevation to canonical status was largely a retrospective construction of the 1840s–1860s nationalist movements. (4) The pirate fleet size estimates (1808-020) vary considerably across sources; the figure of 70,000+ is at the high end and derives partly from Qing official reports that may have inflated numbers to justify military expenditure. (5) The significance of the Sened-i İttifak (1808-009) as an 'Ottoman Magna Carta' is debated — some scholars argue it was too short-lived and too limited in scope to represent a genuine constitutional moment.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1808 is rich but heavily skewed toward European perspectives, particularly the Napoleonic Wars. The Peninsular War generates an enormous volume of documentation from French, British, Spanish, and Portuguese archives, but events in Asia, Africa, and the pre-independence Americas are comparatively underrepresented in English-language scholarship. Ottoman sources exist but are filtered through later compilations. Qing records are extensive but court-centred. The year's documentation reflects the biases of a period when European imperial expansion was generating vast archival records while simultaneously disrupting or ignoring indigenous knowledge systems. Spanish American events are increasingly well-studied thanks to postcolonial historiography, but the African interior and much of Southeast and Central Asia remain poorly covered for this specific year.

graph_edges.note: US slave trade ban enacted in 1807, took effect 1 January 1808 per constitutional provision
Transfer of Portuguese court to Brazil set in motion the political conditions for Brazilian independence in 1822
The Bayonne abdications directly provoked the Dos de Mayo uprising and national insurrection
Crisis of Spanish royal legitimacy triggered junta movements across Spanish America
The Madrid uprising catalysed the formation of provincial juntas and armies that fought at Bailén
The defeat at Bailén forced Napoleon to intervene personally in Spain in November 1808
Erfurt's failure to secure genuine Russian commitment foreshadowed the Franco-Russian breach leading to the 1812 invasion
Finnish War concluded with the Treaty of Fredrikshamn (1809), ceding Finland to Russia
Mahmud II's accession began the reform trajectory that culminated in the destruction of the Janissaries in 1826
The 1808 junta movements provided the ideological and legal framework for the full independence revolts of 1810
Dalton's atomic theory and Davy's elemental discoveries were contemporaneous breakthroughs in British chemistry
US slave trade ban and Sierra Leone Crown Colony status both took effect on 1 January 1808, reflecting the broader Anglo-American anti-slave-trade movement
